---
title: "Guidance for better methods: evaluating infectious disease forecasts"
subtitle: "NIHR Better Methods Better Research Programme — ref 2026/414"
author: "Kath Sherratt, Sam Abbott, Sebastian Funk"
date: today
format: html
bibliography: references.bib
---

# 1. Background and Rationale

## 1.1 The problem: forecast evaluation without standards

## 1.2 Why now

## 1.3 The gap this guidance will address

# 2. Aims and Objectives

## 2.1 Research question

How should infectious disease forecasts be evaluated to support operational public health decision-making, and what guidance would enable consistent best practice across UK settings?

## 2.2 Key aims

This project has three aims.

1. To assess current forecast evaluation practice and capacity across UK public health.
   Effective guidance must begin from an honest account of what practitioners actually do, not only what methodologists recommend.
   We will synthesise existing evidence on metric use across UK forecasting programmes and conduct a knowledge, attitudes, and practices (KAP) survey of public health intelligence professionals to establish a baseline and identify the highest-priority gaps.

2. To develop consensus-based guidance on forecast evaluation that is accessible to non-specialist users.
   Drawing on the needs assessment, we will convene a structured stakeholder process to reach consensus on minimum standards for forecast evaluation.
   The guidance will address which metrics are appropriate for which use cases, how to aggregate performance across forecast targets, and how to communicate evaluation results to decision-makers who are not themselves methodologists.

3. To produce and embed accessible guidance for lasting impact.
   We will publish an open-access, web-based guidance resource, integrate recommendations into the `scoringutils` R package, and work directly with UKHSA and NHS forecasting programmes to support adoption.

## 2.3 Objectives

1. **Evidence synthesis and needs assessment.** Map current metric use against best practice via a documentary synthesis of Forecast Hub evaluations and UKHSA operational reports; and conduct a knowledge, attitudes, and practices (KAP) survey of public health intelligence professionals in NHS trusts and local authorities to establish baseline evaluation capacity and identify priority gaps.

2. **Stakeholder consensus.** Conduct structured workshops and a Nominal Group Technique consensus process among methodologists, operational public health professionals (including UKHSA and NHS trust analysts), and an existing patient and public involvement panel to reach consensus on a minimum standard for forecast evaluation in different public health settings.

3. **Guidance production and embedding.** Develop an accessible, web-based guidance resource with a decision framework for metric selection, worked examples, and companion code; integrate into `scoringutils`; share directly with UKHSA and NHS forecasting programmes; and submit for inclusion in relevant reporting standards networks.

# 3. Methodology

## 3.1 Study design

This project uses a sequential mixed-methods design across three work packages, delivered within 12 months.
Each work package produces both a direct output and evidence that informs the next stage.

**Work Package 1 (months 1-4): Evidence synthesis and needs assessment.** We will map current evaluation practice and assess capacity for change across UK public health forecasting.
This work package has two components.

First, a documentary evidence synthesis mapping current metric use against available best practice, drawing on published evaluations from international Forecast Hubs, UKHSA operational reports, and available grey literature such as NHS Trust guidance.

Second, a knowledge, attitudes, and practices (KAP) survey of public health intelligence professionals in NHS trusts and local authorities.
The survey will establish baseline prevalence of forecast evaluation activity, the metrics currently in use, confidence in interpreting evaluation outputs, and perceived barriers to adopting standardised approaches.
As a survey of professionals in their occupational capacity rather than as NHS patients or research participants, this falls under LSHTM ethics review (not NHS REC), with approval sought in month 1.
The KAP baseline will serve a dual purpose: it will identify the highest-priority gaps to address in the consensus process, and it will provide a pre-guidance benchmark against which adoption and change can be measured after the project ends.

**Work Package 2 (months 4-9): Stakeholder consensus process.** Using findings from WP1, we will convene a Nominal Group Technique (NGT) consensus process.
Consensus will be sought on a minimum standard set of evaluation metrics, guidance on metric selection by use case, and the format and scope of the final guidance resource.
NGT is well-suited to this project: it is faster than Delphi (typically two to three rounds over weeks rather than months), structured to prevent dominant voices shaping outcomes, and produces prioritised, actionable recommendations.
The panel will include methodologists, operational public health professionals, and public and patient representatives from the existing HPRU-HAM PPI panel.

**Work Package 3 (months 8-12): Guidance production, dissemination, and embedding.** Overlapping with the final stages of WP2, we will develop the guidance resource in parallel with consensus finalisation.
The output will be an open-access, web-based resource structured for progressive complexity: from conceptual orientation for non-specialists, through a decision framework for metric selection, to worked examples with companion code.
We will integrate recommendations into the `scoringutils` R package documentation and share outputs directly with UKHSA and NHS forecasting programme leads for adoption.

## 3.2 Developing the guidance: methodological approach

The guidance will address a practical problem that sits at the boundary between statistical methodology and operational decision-making.
Its design must therefore satisfy two audiences who have different needs: methodologists who require technical rigour, and public health practitioners who require clarity and usability without specialist statistical training.

The structure of the guidance will follow a decision-oriented logic, organised by use case rather than by metric.
A practitioner selecting metrics to evaluate a short-term flu forecast for NHS winter planning has different needs from one evaluating a multi-pathogen outbreak forecast for national risk stratification.
The guidance will map these contexts explicitly, drawing on the typology of forecast use cases developed in WP1 and WP2.

For each use case, the guidance will specify: which metrics are appropriate and why; what the metric does and does not capture; how to interpret results for non-specialist audiences; and how to implement evaluation using `scoringutils`.
Worked examples will be drawn from UK public health forecasting programmes, with permission from the originating organisations.

A web-based format is central as static documents (PDFs, journal supplements) become outdated and are not consulted in practice.
For example, the `epinowcast` guide to reporting delays for sub-national health teams demonstrates that a well-structured, maintained web resource becomes a reference point for practitioners and a living document that can be updated as methods evolve.
We will adopt the same model: Quarto-based, version-controlled, open to community contribution, and structured for scannable navigation alongside deeper reading.

## 3.3 Identifying stakeholders

## 3.4 Eliciting stakeholder views and co-producing guidance

## 3.5 Consensus process: scope and format

## 3.6 Outcome measures

## 3.7 Timeline (see Gantt chart)

## 3.8 Measures of success

## 3.9 Risks and mitigation

# 4. Inclusive Research

## 4.1 Equality, diversity, and inclusion in design

## 4.2 Participant recruitment

## 4.3 Data and analysis

## 4.4 Knowledge mobilisation and dissemination

# 5. Knowledge Mobilisation, Dissemination and Impact

## 5.1 Outputs

## 5.2 Engaging stakeholders, patients, and the public

## 5.3 Entry into health and care systems

## 5.4 Barriers to adoption and mitigation

## 5.5 Anticipated impact

## 5.6 Sharing findings with participants

# 6. Project Management

## 6.1 Management structure

## 6.2 Roles of applicants

# 7. Research Governance and Ethical Approval

# 8. Team Expertise

## 8.1 Team member contributions

## 8.2 PPI lead

# 9. Intellectual Property and Commercialisation

# Budget

## Salary costs

## Expenses
